speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You were talking about hiding in the trenches.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trenches, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What happened from that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rain was a heavy rain. It filled up the trenches till here. Then it's no-- there was no other way. And the shooting stopped a little bit. Still, I hear shooting stopped. Then I went out from that water. I was feeling cold this time. And I was-- this time was the end from fighting. We all have to turn back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was later on, they told me that it was 1,000 soldiers that got killed. And 3,000 came out in that place where I was there. Then we start-- we have to pull back. We went on the road through Silesia, Katowice, Myslowice. We went on the road to Krakow. It was there a road, a good road. And we were walking and walking and walking. We came-- before Krakow, it's a little bit a hill there. Then the army decided, the officers decided, to fight again back, to hold the Germans back. Then it was maybe a division or maybe more, maybe 20,000 soldiers. And we started to fight before Krakow back to holding. Only it didn't help, because they got like we have here, planes, tanks, trucks, like here in the United States. And the Polish army didn't got nothing. They got horses and wagons. Then we saw that the war was lost, that we go-- sooner or later, any day, we're going to lose the war. And then over there, they killed a lot of Polish soldiers. And one officer who was on the [INAUDIBLE], because I remembered him when he got all of his speeches, you know, in front of the soldiers-- then he was talking about Jewish people, politics, that the Jewish people-- like the anti-Semites are talking. Only this time, I was with a few soldiers-- the Jewish people. Only we-- we were afraid to ask him questions, because he was an officer, you know, with a gun in his pocket. We didn't say nothing, you know, in the army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only why I mention this, when I was in Krakow there, he calls me with the others-- not the other soldier, me. Steinweis, come over here. You're going on a patrol. And he said to me, then I asked him only one word, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] somebody goes with me, in Polish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He took his a gun. He put to my head. One more word, I kill you, he said. When I say something, you have not to say nothing. I didn't say nothing. And then he took another fellow. And he went me-- with me on a patrol. On the road there, we were hiding, too. You could not go too far, because the Germans, they were all over the soldiers. Like, I met a colonel.

speaker: interviewee
text: This time, I didn't know that he is a colonel. And he said, where are you running? And I said, I want to avoid that they have not to kill me. I have too-- he said listen, it will not help running.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans are all over. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lost the war, he said to me, a colonel. And he went with us some. And I think he was a Jewish colonel, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we went further up. We were walking and walking. Then we-- we got lost. I didn't see anymore of the officers. I didn't see nobody, only what I see soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't know where we are. Then we were talking with the soldiers-- the few Jewish soldiers. What we-- what we have to do now? They told me, we're going to the Russian border. Maybe we can go to the Russian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, I went with them I think for 10 hours, maybe. Only, it was far to the Russian border. And then we were very tired. We went in to a farmer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Something to eat? And the farmer gave us two chickens. One soldier killed the chickens. And then one cooked. And we got our supper with the potatoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we fall asleep, all of us. And when we got up, the Germans are surrounded. I got my rifle. I didn't take my rifle. I was there till maybe 10 o'clock in the morning. And then the woman-- the farmer said-- said to me, listen. When they will catch you here-- when you have rifles here or there and here, they will come. And they will kill you on the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, what I can do now? You have to decide what to do. You are a soldier. The answer was right. And I decided to go out on the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other soldiers didn't go. They were afraid. I said, I don't have a rifle. I don't have nothing. Then maybe they will talk to me. I don't know. And I went out in the street. It was in a village. They called this [NON-ENGLISH]. And I went there on the road. And I see maybe 200 soldiers are coming, the front white horses with the-- with the officer in the front with his gun in his hand. And I'm going. And he-- he stopped me when I-- I went in the side. And he went with the horse in the middle. And behind was maybe 200 horses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN] in German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told him, I will not say that I am from Sosnowiec or [INAUDIBLE]. I told him I am from Katowice. [SPEAKING GERMAN]? I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewee
text: He said to me, go home. He thought that I'm-- I'm maybe from Silesia. The Silesian people, they were half Polacks and half Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before, there were Germans. When Poland occupied around 1920 or 1918, and then they became-- like even the language, they didn't talk clean the language. They talked like half German and half Polish. And I went further. And then I stopped by a farmer. He said, he gave me food not to be there, because I was wearing the Polish uniform. And I went further. And I figured out better what night I'm home. It was far to go home. Then I got the other fellow, a Polish [INAUDIBLE] Polish-- Polack soldier-- Polish soldier. He said, you know, I like you. You're a good man. Maybe I will walk with you. I said, that's OK. You can walk with me. And it was maybe a 1 o'clock, midnight, in the woods. A patrol, a German patrol, caught us. You know, they're going to shoot us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Oh, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. From where-- from where you are? I said, I'm from Katowice. And Katowice was not far from Sosnowiec, only 3 kilometers. And he was a nice man. He said, sit down. You want a cigarette, or you want some food? He gave me everything. And who is the other fellow? I said, he's a Polish soldier. He speaks German? I said no.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then first of all, when they caught us, they searched the pockets and everything. They didn't get nothing, only the soldier got bullets in the pockets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they came out. The soldier, he said, he got bullets. You have something to do with them. And he was begging. He talked to me-- he talked to me Polish. I have to explain to them. And I said, he didn't know that he got the bullets in the pockets. And he's a very nice man. And he don't have a rifle. He can nothing do with the bullets. Then they took away the bullets. And everything was OK. And I was sitting with them maybe an hour and a half, two hours. He said, they let me go. You want to go home, go home. Only when I went further, then some other patrol, German, caught me. And this time, I didn't get it out. I was waiting till it would get daytime. I was sitting there in-- in a place.

speaker: interviewee
text: And daytime, they took me into a table in the woods. And over there was two officers. And there was maybe 10 other soldiers. And I was there. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he said to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? You understand what this means? Oh, I am Jewish. And I said to him, yeah. I never said that I'm not Jewish. No matter where I was, when a German asked me I am Jewish, I am Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. He was going to shot me right away. And I see the other 9, 10, Polacks, they're putting aside. And nothing will happen to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came, the other officer. He said, why are you staying there? We're going to shoot him. And he said, he is a prison of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is against the law, Jew or not Jew, to shoot. He-- he-- he will go to be a prison of war from that-- from that minute. And forget about what you were saying. And right way, he put me in among the other soldiers. And it was another 10, another 10. We had maybe 30, 40 soldiers. And they took us away to a school I remember. And from there, they took us another place. And then they took us to the station there with the name from the city, Staszow. They loaded us in maybe, oh, I would say-- because it was not regular. It was cattle wagons, the train. And inside was nothing, like they carry horses or cows. And we went in maybe 30, 35, or 40 in-- in one wagon. And it was maybe-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say maybe 40 wagons. It was a few thousand people they put in there. And I came in that wagon was six doctors, Jewish people, doctors. Because when the war broke out, anybody who got college educated, he became right away an officer. A doctor, when he went-- they took him in the army, right away he was officer. They were all officers. And it was the other people only soldiers. And we went in that wagon, that-- that transport, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe five or six days with interruption. They stopped the train. They gave us a little bit of food, bread, water they got. They gave us not something to drink, warm. So where-- and they-- took us into [INAUDIBLE] by [INAUDIBLE] and that little town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this town you just left from, was that Rzeszow? The place that we just came from was-- that was the Rzeszow? Was that what you told me about before or no? Before STALAG-- before you went to the STALAG, you were, for five days in Poland, at where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five days in Poland?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, before you went to [INAUDIBLE] by [INAUDIBLE], weren't you some place else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I came in to the transfer to [INAUDIBLE] by [INAUDIBLE]. I came in with the transport there. They were loaded us. And they put us in. It was the building-- not-finished buildings. And it was a building there. It was-- what-- German officers worked there in the building. And we were 10,000 soldiers already there when I came. And-- and this time, it was maybe a week later. We got very little food. In the morning, we got some bread. And then-- then later, in the afternoon, they gave us soup, other piece of bread. And that's all what we got. And they separated us-- the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the-- from the not-Jewish soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this at [INAUDIBLE] by [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Everything was there in [INAUDIBLE] by [INAUDIBLE] in that town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was STALAG V A?

speaker: interviewee
text: V A, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was life like there in STALAG V A? What was-- what was it like in a POW camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over there, it was their army, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a [SPEAKING GERMAN], they called this. Not far, soldiers were there, maybe-- maybe 2,000. Then it was a building. And then it was six building, not finished building. And we are 600 Jewish prisoners of war, soldiers, Jewish soldiers and 9,400. I know exactly, because I heard the Germans saying. Once when he got a speech, he said, you are here, 10,000 prisoners of war. So we were 600, was 9,400 left. And they separated us. We were living in the same building. It was a big building, even bigger than this-- longer. And we were living in a separate place, in a big room, the 600. And it was not so good there. Then once, I-- I walked around among 10,000 soldiers. And we didn't got nothing to do, nothing even to read, nothing to hear. Only, we talked to each other. Then I saw a man he's walking, a civilian, among the 10,000 soldiers [INAUDIBLE] his hat. And he said something. And everybody's making the hands like this, like this. They don't understand him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came near. And I said to him, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. What do you want? He said, I'm looking for a worker. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. Come on over here. He said everything in German he said. And I came over to him. He said, I need another worker. I need two workers. One, I'll put in the kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you will work in general stuff. I said, me in the general stuff? What will I do there? And I am Jewish, I said to him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You are not Jewish. I-- I said what I will do when they will ask me I am Jewish? You are not Jewish. This was the answer. Do you want to work? Or, you don't want to work? I said, I want to work. Why not? I have a chance, I will work. Right away, I called the other man.

speaker: interviewee
text: What was-- I knew him from home. And I put him in the kitchen. He was a butcher. He was putting him in, not me. I introduced them. And now he said, you're going with me. He takes me in that building. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. I was laughing already on the way. He said [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And I remember what I'm saying. I'm coming in there. Officers are sitting working by the desk. It was there maybe-- it was a few floors, maybe four or five floors. Everybody got his office, his desk. And they're working on papers. He said, your work will be, you will clean the ashtray with a vacuum cleaner. You go over every day in morning here. And then you'll go upstairs on a floor higher. And this is your work. And nobody comes here, only one soldier. And this is you. And I worked there till-- til I went home. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Later on, I will tell you how I came home and why they sent us home. Only when I worked there, I-- I got already food plenty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even the officers-- the German officers, they gave me anything. They asked me, oh, I need money? I said, I don't need money. I need food when they asked me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And later on, I didn't want food from them, because my boss he was a Democrat. He was not a Nazi. He got two educated son officers in the German army. And he was a religious Protestant man. He said, when I treat you good, God will take care of my sons. And he-- he was a nice man, a very nice man. He took me home. I took showers for him. He gave me breakfast.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I said to him, you know, my Herr, I have food. I have-- I'm happy here now. Only my comrades, they don't have food. He said, what do you think I can do for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, when you take me in the [NON-ENGLISH] there-- where they lived, the soldiers, the German soldiers, I will sweep the floor there. I work there. Only they're throwing away every day-- I knew before-- bread in the barrels. And those barrels' bread, I want for my comrades. We can arrange that, he said. And he did it. In another few days, came a few guys-- my friends. And they took it bags-- from the barrels in the backs. And they took it in there, in the lager, in the camp. And they went. And we used to have a [SPEAKING GERMAN], a leader from the 600. Somebody's got to be. He was-- I think he was an officer. Now, he didn't want to know nobody that he's an officer. He was an educated man. Because when he was the officer, they took the officers away someplace else. I don't know whether they survived or not. I don't know. Then he was just a plain soldier like we are. And he was a very nice man. And he organized that every day all prisoners got separate food from me and from the German. He knew it, that German, everything. And then, when it was left in the kitchen, soup, often, dinners that they cooked for the prisoners, he'd let me know. And I told my leader. And they sent in a few people. And they brought soup or some other food. And this was till the end. And then the end came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in February. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then they annexed-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the German annexed-- till Krakow. Forever, we will belong this to-- this to Germany, the land everything. And I am from Sosnowiec, [INAUDIBLE]. This is right on the borderline from what used to be the German border. And we were 600. 300, there were people from there till Krakow. From my town maybe was 10, maybe 15.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other 300, they were from Lwow, Lemberg, from the other side there, from Galicia over there not far from Russia. With those people, what happened? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: The 300, when we left, we went home by train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I remember I was in the train by myself. It happened so I was by myself. Maybe it was another soldier or two on the end there. And came in an officer, a German officer. And I was supposed to say, good morning, Herr-- to-- to stay in salute to him. Only, I was sitting on a bench there. I didn't say nothing. I was sitting. And he beat me up, because I didn't get up and say hello to him or to make. And I had told him only one thing, I didn't see you. I was sleeping there in the corner. I didn't see you. Otherwise, I would do that. And he-- he threatened me whether he would throw me out from the train. Only he didn't throw me out. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: This train was heading to where? This train was going where? Back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Lublin, to Lublin. And then we came to Lublin. It was maybe, I would say, 2 an 1/2 miles or 3 miles from the station till that camp Majdanek. Only, Majdanek later became like Auschwitz. Only in this time, they were building already. Before, was horses there. And they-- they-- they started to build, to make the camp bigger. Then I came there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I was very thirsty. I was feeling something is cooking there in the side. And I'm going there and knock on the door. A Yiddish-- a Jewish girl opens the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, what I can do for you? I said, I want water. She gave me a water. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then she said, nothing will happen to you. I said, what's got to happen to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: She told me, listen. I don't have-- I-- I can't talk to you too long or too much. Only I will say they stopped shooting the prisoners. Before I came-- a day before-- they took the Jewish prisoners to [INAUDIBLE]. And they shot all of them there. From my town, my friend was shot there. When I came home, they told me-- they told me he was shot in [INAUDIBLE]. The Jewish [INAUDIBLE], the committee in Lublin, they were very active. They went door by door to Jewish people in the whole city. And they took everything-- not but first, they were begging them to give gold, diamonds, money-- everything what they have to stop shooting the soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stop right there. And we'll take a break for a second.
